description:
You are a machine-learning engineer who just received two raw JSON data files and a JSON-Schema describing the expected record format.  
    Your goal is to validate every record, discard the invalid ones, and prepare a clean, newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) file ready for model ingestion, plus a concise validation report.

    Starting file layout (already present when the session begins):
    1. /home/user/ml_data/raw/training_v1.json  
    2. /home/user/ml_data/raw/training_v2.json  
    3. /home/user/ml_data/schema/record_schema.json  

    Task details
    1. Create the directory /home/user/ml_data/processed/  if it does not yet exist.  
    2. Validate every object in both source files against the schema in /home/user/ml_data/schema/record_schema.json.  
       • A record is valid if and only if it contains BOTH keys “text” and “label”, each mapped to a JSON string, and **no additional keys**.  
    3. Write all valid records to /home/user/ml_data/processed/clean_training.ndjson using the following strict format rules:  
       • NDJSON: one and only one JSON object per line (no outer array, no blank lines, no leading/trailing spaces).  
       • Each object must be rendered in its most compact form (i.e. {"k":"v","k2":"v2"} with no extra whitespace).  
       • Record order: keep the original order in which the valid records appear when the input files are processed in lexicographic file-name order (training_v1.json first, then training_v2.json).  
    4. Produce a human-readable log named /home/user/ml_data/processed/validation_report.log that exactly follows the template below (including line breaks and punctuation).  Every numeric value must be correct.  

       FILE_VALIDATION_SUMMARY  
       -----------------------  
       Total_Files: <number_of_files_processed>  
       Total_Records: <total_records_seen>  
       Valid_Records: <valid_records_kept>  
       Invalid_Records: <invalid_records_discarded>  

       Invalid_Details:  
       <file_name>:<1_based_index_of_invalid_record_in_that_file>  
       <file_name>:<1_based_index_of_invalid_record_in_that_file>  
       … (one line per invalid record)  

       Notes on the template:  
       • “1_based_index_of_invalid_record_in_that_file” means the position of the record in the original array of that particular file, counting from 1.  
       • There must be no extra lines before or after the block.  

    Verification checklist (all must pass for the task to be considered complete):  
    a. /home/user/ml_data/processed/clean_training.ndjson exists.  
    b. It contains exactly the valid records, each on its own line, in the required order, with exactly 7 lines in total.  
    c. /home/user/ml_data/processed/validation_report.log exists and matches the template and the correct numeric values.  
    d. No invalid record appears in the NDJSON file.  

    Hints (you are free to ignore these):  
    • jq can compact-encode JSON objects with the “-c” flag.  
    • You do not need any external network access; everything is local.  
    • Python’s “jsonschema” module or jq’s type checks are both viable for validation.  
    • Keep command count reasonable (5-10), e.g. create dir, validate/filter with jq, redirect output, build counts with jq/wc, echo the report.
